titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 26 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/74

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Nov. 26. My p r. No letter last night from you tho' a nice little scrap from
Jessie which was of course a good substitute. Please thank her.
Perhaps it will come midday with the ordinary letters. I think the
censor must have a special predilection for your letters & don't blame
him if so. It hasn't come the result Last night I was up till midnight having had a busy day
with our move. I just glanced at the paper, read Redmond's speech,
& promptly seized my pen & wrote to Carson. If the tolerance shown
by our men — to the nationalists is merely to be used as a means
of exploiting Home Rule, there will be a row, & I shall not stop it,
I have suggested to Carson that we should write a counterblast. Reallly we want him as dictator, the Govt seems more weak kneed
than ever ! The 107th Brigade has been doing very well at the front, &
everyone is finding fault in this Division with our its substitutes, so
that the General now seems keen to get them back, I fear more
so than they are to come ! The 14th got badly strafed yesterday. Major P.Kerr
Smiley is not a great commander & the Gen' Hickman now thinks Col C would
be better employed elsewhere, having realized that he won't go
down with the General when he comes, so I think he won't come
after all. I think I should write to Mr J Cunningham if you can make
it quite clear to him that you want advice not a subscription You have
the army list. I could have
thought out such a polite letter for you which would have made them writhe. I am thrilled about the flat in Sheffield Terrace. Lady K was
not the Co Down one I suppose wife of Sir Robert of that ilk? The Spectator on Thursday will be quite in time. I usually
see Russell's Times so don't do so badly now. I went to the bombing of the Brigade Bombers whom I have
been coaching, & the Gen' was really pleased for once. They did better
than I expected. You would have been amusedto day to see a horse looking
out over the road from a room upstairs. The horses are many of them
billeted in semi ruined houses & in this case this was a house built into
the side of a hill so that the first floor was level with the ground & the
horses walked in on to it. The wall was a bit broken & an old horse
looked out with much complacency. I am longing for further news of your Com'meemeetings &
plans, but your letters have a way of arriving one day late as far
as I can make out. Would you put the time of posting on occasionally. It
is obviously wrong for Tuesday's paper to come before your Monday's letter, &
I wonder if they get delayed en route. If so the Censor must often
learn something curious about the natural history of squirrels & a p rabbit. I think you would do well to encourage Mater to think of going
to an chemist later. Had we been at Belfast I would certainly have
suggested DrCraig. Best love to "you all" (how does P.R like this ) Wilfrid BSpender
